update_summary: Initial release — 2025 Code of Good Practice: Dismissal

legal_currency.primary_code: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal, GG 53294, 4 September 2025
legal_currency.bcea: Basic Conditions of Employment Act 75 of 1997
legal_currency.popia: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013
legal_currency.constitution: Constitution of the Republic of South Africa, 1996

absenteeism.label: Absenteeism — unauthorised absence
absenteeism.standard_wording: That the employee was absent from work without the permission of the employer and without a valid reason on [DATE(S)], thereby contravening [clause X of the Disciplinary Code / the employer's attendance policy / the implied term of the contract of employment].
absenteeism.particulars: Insert: specific date(s) of absence; whether any notice was given; whether a medical certificate was produced; whether prior warnings exist for same conduct.
absenteeism.severity: First offence: written warning or final written warning depending on duration and pattern. Persistent or extended unauthorised absence: dismissal may be appropriate. Note: distinguish absenteeism (misconduct) from ill-health absence (incapacity — different process).
absenteeism.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9; CCMA Guidelines on Misconduct Arbitrations

insubordination.label: Insubordination — refusal of lawful instruction
insubordination.standard_wording: That the employee refused to carry out a lawful and reasonable instruction given by [NAME AND DESIGNATION] on [DATE], without just cause, thereby undermining the authority of management and contravening [clause X of the Disciplinary Code].
insubordination.particulars: Insert: exact instruction given; who gave it; when and how given; that it was lawful; that employee refused; reason given if any.
insubordination.severity: Single refusal: written or final written warning. Wilful, deliberate or contemptuous refusal; repeated refusal; refusal combined with threatening conduct: dismissal may be appropriate even first offence.
insubordination.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9

dishonesty.label: Dishonesty — theft, fraud, misrepresentation
dishonesty.standard_wording: That the employee, on or about [DATE], dishonestly and without the consent of the employer [took / misappropriated / failed to account for / misrepresented] [describe property, funds, or information] belonging to or in the custody of the employer, to the value of approximately [AMOUNT], thereby breaching the trust placed in the employee and contravening [clause X of the Disciplinary Code].
dishonesty.particulars: Insert: precise description; date and place; how discovered; admission or denial; restitution offered.
dishonesty.severity: Dismissal generally appropriate even for first offence. Gross dishonesty renders the continued employment relationship intolerable. Even small-value theft can justify dismissal where trust is fundamentally breached.
dishonesty.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9(1)(a); Edcon Ltd v Pillemer NO (2010) 31 ILJ 340 (SCA)

gross_negligence.label: Gross negligence
gross_negligence.standard_wording: That the employee, on [DATE], failed to exercise the standard of care and diligence reasonably expected of a person in the position of [JOB TITLE], in that [describe the specific act or omission], which resulted in [describe the harm, loss, or risk caused], in contravention of [clause X of the Disciplinary Code / the employee's duty of care].
gross_negligence.particulars: Insert: specific standard expected; how breached; harm or potential harm; whether wilful or careless; prior training or warnings given.
gross_negligence.severity: Gross negligence (reckless disregard for consequences): dismissal may be appropriate even first offence, particularly where serious harm or significant loss resulted. Ordinary negligence: progressive discipline more appropriate.
gross_negligence.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9

assault.label: Assault / workplace violence
assault.standard_wording: That the employee, on [DATE] at [LOCATION], unlawfully and intentionally assaulted / threatened / physically attacked [NAME AND DESIGNATION OF COMPLAINANT], by [describe the act], in contravention of [clause X of the Disciplinary Code] and the employer's duty to provide a safe working environment in terms of the Occupational Health and Safety Act 85 of 1993.
assault.particulars: Insert: names of parties; exact nature of the act; witnesses; whether criminal charge laid; whether victim required medical attention; prior history of violent conduct.
assault.severity: Physical assault: generally warrants dismissal for first offence. Verbal threats: final written warning for first offence escalating to dismissal on repetition. Consider OHSA duty of care.
assault.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9; Occupational Health and Safety Act 85 of 1993, section 8

sexual_harassment.label: Sexual harassment
sexual_harassment.standard_wording: That the employee, on [DATE(S)], subjected [NAME] to unwanted conduct of a sexual nature, including [describe specific conduct: verbal, physical, non-verbal, quid pro quo, or hostile work environment conduct], which constitutes sexual harassment in terms of the Code of Good Practice on the Handling of Sexual Harassment Cases in the Workplace, and contravenes [clause X of the Disciplinary Code] and the Employment Equity Act 55 of 1998.
sexual_harassment.particulars: Insert: nature of conduct; whether reported previously; complainant's response; witnesses or documentary evidence; whether repeated.
sexual_harassment.severity: Serious sexual harassment (physical; persistent; abuse of authority; quid pro quo): dismissal appropriate first offence. Less serious isolated conduct: final written warning. Employer must apply EEA section 60 obligations.
sexual_harassment.authority: Employment Equity Act 55 of 1998, section 6; Code of Good Practice on Handling of Sexual Harassment Cases in the Workplace

substance_abuse.label: Substance abuse — intoxication
substance_abuse.standard_wording: That the employee, on [DATE], reported for duty and/or was found to be under the influence of alcohol [or any other intoxicating substance] while on duty, in contravention of [clause X of the Disciplinary Code], as evidenced by [describe: behaviour observed; breathalyser reading; witness observations].
substance_abuse.particulars: Insert: how intoxication identified; breathalyser reading if applicable; whether employee acknowledged or denied; whether employee has known alcohol dependency (if so, incapacity process may be more appropriate).
substance_abuse.severity: Distinguish misconduct (choice to consume) from incapacity (addiction). Safety-critical environments: dismissal may be warranted first offence. Non-safety environments: graduated discipline unless egregious or prior warnings exist.
substance_abuse.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9

wilful_damage.label: Wilful damage to property
wilful_damage.standard_wording: That the employee, on [DATE], wilfully and without justification damaged / destroyed property belonging to or in the custody of the employer, namely [describe item], with an estimated replacement / repair value of [AMOUNT], in contravention of [clause X of the Disciplinary Code].
wilful_damage.particulars: Insert: description of property; how damaged; estimated monetary value; deliberate or reckless; witnesses; restitution offered or required.
wilful_damage.severity: Wilful damage: generally gross misconduct justifying dismissal. Accidental damage should not be charged as misconduct — address through counselling unless gross negligence applicable.
wilful_damage.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9

confidentiality.label: Breach of confidentiality
confidentiality.standard_wording: That the employee, on or about [DATE], without authorisation disclosed / communicated / made available confidential information belonging to the employer, namely [describe information], to [describe the recipient or platform], in breach of [the confidentiality clause in the employment contract / clause X of the Disciplinary Code / the Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013].
confidentiality.particulars: Insert: nature of confidential information; how disclosed; to whom; intentional or reckless; whether any harm or potential harm resulted.
confidentiality.severity: Intentional disclosure of commercially sensitive, personal, or strategic information: dismissal appropriate. Careless disclosure without intent to harm: final written warning with remedial training.
confidentiality.authority: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013; Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9

gross_insubordination.label: Gross insubordination — contemptuous behaviour
gross_insubordination.standard_wording: That the employee, on [DATE], in the presence of [witnesses], directed abusive, contemptuous, and/or threatening language and/or conduct towards [NAME AND DESIGNATION], a person in authority over the employee, by [describe specific words or conduct], in contravention of [clause X of the Disciplinary Code].
gross_insubordination.particulars: Insert: exact words or conduct (record verbatim where possible); context; who was present; whether provoked; prior warnings for similar conduct.
gross_insubordination.severity: Contemptuous, abusive, or threatening conduct directed at management: final written warning or dismissal depending on severity, prior record, and provocation. Distinguish from forceful disagreement (acceptable) — the manner, not the fact of disagreement, is the issue.
gross_insubordination.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9

unauthorised_use.label: Unauthorised use of employer resources
unauthorised_use.standard_wording: That the employee, on [DATE(S)], without the permission of the employer, used employer property / resources / systems, namely [describe: vehicle, computer, telephone, tools, data], for private and/or unauthorised purposes, in contravention of [clause X of the Disciplinary Code / the employer's acceptable use policy].
unauthorised_use.particulars: Insert: description of resource used; purpose for which used; extent of use; whether similar use had previously been condoned by employer.
unauthorised_use.severity: Consider whether conduct was previously condoned — condonation may be raised as a defence. Isolated or minor unauthorised use: written warning. Extensive, dishonest, or financially significant misuse: final written warning or dismissal.
unauthorised_use.authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 9

hearing_steps.title: Opening & introductions
hearing_steps.description: Introduce all parties, confirm the chairperson's role and independence, inform the employee of representation rights.
checklist: Chairperson introduced by name and role — confirmed independent
All parties present identified and recorded
Employee informed of right to representation (fellow employee or shop steward)
Representative confirmed and recorded
Employee asked whether they received the notice of hearing
Employee asked whether they understand the charge
Interpreter arranged if required
Employee confirms adequate time to prepare
Preliminary objections called for and dealt with
prompts: I am [NAME]. I have been appointed as the independent chairperson of this hearing. I have no personal interest in the outcome and will conduct this hearing fairly.
You have the right to be represented by a fellow employee or shop steward of your choice. Who will be representing you today?
Do you confirm that you have received the notice of hearing and the charge sheet? Do you understand the charge against you?
Do you have any preliminary objections to the procedure before we proceed?

hearing_steps.title: Reading the charge & plea
hearing_steps.description: Read the charge aloud clearly. Obtain the employee's plea on record.
checklist: Charge read aloud slowly and clearly
Charge explained in plain terms if required
Employee asked to enter plea — guilty or not guilty
Plea recorded on the hearing record
If guilty plea — confirmed freely and voluntarily made
prompts: I will now read the charge against you. Please listen carefully.
How do you plead — guilty or not guilty?
I record that the employee has entered a plea of [GUILTY / NOT GUILTY].

hearing_steps.title: Employer's case — evidence
hearing_steps.description: Initiator presents the employer's case through witnesses and documentary evidence.
checklist: Initiator invited to present employer's case
Each employer witness called and testified
Employee offered opportunity to cross-examine each employer witness
Chairperson asked clarifying questions where necessary
Documentary evidence received and numbered as exhibits
Employer confirmed case is closed
prompts: The employer will now present its case. Please call your first witness.
Does the employee or their representative wish to question this witness?
I have a few questions of my own for this witness.
Are there any further witnesses for the employer? Is the employer's case now closed?

hearing_steps.title: Employee's case — response
hearing_steps.description: Employee presents their version, calls witnesses, and produces documents.
checklist: Employee informed of right to present their version and call witnesses
Employee testified or elected not to testify (recorded)
Employee's witnesses called and testified
Initiator offered opportunity to cross-examine employee's witnesses
Employee's documentary evidence received and numbered
Employee confirmed case is closed
prompts: The employee now has an opportunity to respond to the employer's case. You may present your own version, call witnesses, and produce documents.
Does the employer wish to cross-examine this witness?
Is the employee's case now closed?

hearing_steps.title: Closing arguments
hearing_steps.description: Both parties summarise their case and state their desired outcome.
checklist: Employer invited to make closing argument
Employee invited to make closing argument
Key points from both closing arguments recorded
prompts: We have now heard all the evidence. I invite both parties to make a brief closing argument — a summary of your position.
The employer will go first, followed by the employee.

hearing_steps.title: Adjournment to consider finding
hearing_steps.description: Chairperson adjourns to deliberate. Standard: balance of probabilities.
checklist: Hearing adjourned — time and expected reconvening date recorded
Balance of probabilities standard applied (not beyond reasonable doubt)
Credibility of witnesses assessed
Seven-factor test (2025 Code, item 9) applied
Finding on guilt reached before considering sanction
prompts: I am going to adjourn to consider the evidence and reach a finding. I will reconvene at [TIME / DATE].

hearing_steps.title: Delivering the finding on guilt
hearing_steps.description: Announce the finding with reasons. Guilty or not guilty.
checklist: Hearing reconvened — all parties present confirmed
Finding announced — guilty or not guilty
Reasons for finding stated
If not guilty — case closed, matter to be removed from record
If guilty — proceed to sanction stage
prompts: Having considered all the evidence presented by both parties, and applying the balance of probabilities test, I find the employee [GUILTY / NOT GUILTY] of the charge of [state charge].
My reasons are as follows: [brief explanation].

hearing_steps.title: Sanction stage
hearing_steps.description: Hear submissions on mitigating and aggravating factors before imposing sanction.
checklist: Employer invited to present aggravating factors
Employee invited to present mitigating factors
Length of service, prior record, personal circumstances considered
Consistency of sanction with prior comparable cases considered
Progressive discipline considered — could a lesser sanction prevent recurrence?
Actual or potential harm considered (2025 Code, new requirement)
Sanction decided — reasons recorded
prompts: Now that I have made a finding of guilty, I invite both parties to make submissions on the appropriate sanction.
Employer: please present any aggravating factors.
Employee: please present any mitigating factors.

hearing_steps.title: Delivering the sanction & closing
hearing_steps.description: Announce sanction with reasons. Inform employee of CCMA rights.
checklist: Sanction announced with full reasons
For dismissal: effective date stated; notice pay confirmed
For warning: type, period, and implications stated
Employee informed of right to refer to CCMA within 30 days
CCMA referral deadline calculated and recorded
Outcome letter to be issued in writing within [X] days
Hearing formally closed — time recorded
prompts: Having considered the mitigating and aggravating factors, and taking into account the principle of progressive discipline and the consistency requirement, I impose the following sanction: [state sanction with reasons].
You are hereby informed that you have the right to refer this matter to the CCMA if you believe this dismissal or sanction is unfair. Such a referral must be made within 30 days of today.
This hearing is now closed. The time is [TIME].

decision_factors.label: Was there a rule or standard?
decision_factors.guidance: Check whether the rule exists in the written disciplinary code, the employment contract, a workplace policy, a collective agreement, or is a well-understood implied rule. The 2025 Code requires the rule to be both valid AND reasonable.
decision_factors.template_yes: I am satisfied that a rule / standard existed that regulated the employee's conduct. This rule was contained in [the Disciplinary Code / the contract of employment / a workplace policy / was an implied rule of the workplace]. The rule was valid in that it served a legitimate operational purpose and was reasonable in the circumstances of this workplace.
decision_factors.template_no: No clear rule or standard has been established to regulate the conduct alleged. The employer has not shown that [X] was prohibited or that the employee was required to [Y]. This factor weighs in favour of the employee.

decision_factors.label: Was the employee aware of the rule?
decision_factors.guidance: Check whether the employee received the code, attended induction, signed acknowledgement, or whether the conduct is of such obvious wrongfulness that no reasonable person could claim ignorance. Test is objective.
decision_factors.template_yes: I am satisfied that the employee was aware of, or could reasonably have been expected to be aware of, this rule, in that [the employee received a copy of the disciplinary code at induction / signed an acknowledgement of the policy / the rule is of such fundamental and obvious application that no reasonable employee could claim ignorance of it].
decision_factors.template_no: The employee was not shown to have been made aware of this rule. There is no evidence of induction, written acknowledgement, or prior application of this rule. The rule is not of such obvious fundamental nature that awareness can be implied.

decision_factors.label: Was the rule valid and reasonable?
decision_factors.guidance: A rule is valid if it serves a legitimate business purpose and does not require unlawful conduct. A rule is reasonable if a reasonable employer in similar circumstances would apply it. (2025 Code: both elements required, not alternative.)
decision_factors.template_yes: The rule in question is valid and reasonable. It serves the legitimate operational purpose of [describe purpose] and a reasonable employer in similar circumstances would apply such a rule.
decision_factors.template_no: The rule as applied is [not valid / not reasonable] in that it [does not serve a clear business purpose / would not be applied by a reasonable employer in similar circumstances / requires conduct that is unlawful or unfair].

decision_factors.label: Did the employee breach the rule?
decision_factors.guidance: Balance of probabilities — not beyond reasonable doubt. Whose version is more probable? Assess consistency, corroboration, demeanour, inherent probability. Employer bears onus of proof.
decision_factors.template_yes: I find, on a balance of probabilities, that the employee did commit the misconduct as charged. In reaching this finding, I have considered the evidence of all witnesses and the documentary evidence produced. I preferred the employer's version because [it was internally consistent / corroborated by documentary evidence / the opposing version contained material contradictions].
decision_factors.template_no: I find that the breach has not been established on a balance of probabilities. The employer has not discharged the onus of proof. The employee's version is [more probable / equally probable / cannot be excluded as improbable], and doubt must be resolved in the employee's favour.

decision_factors.label: Was the rule consistently applied?
decision_factors.guidance: Check whether other employees who committed the same or similar misconduct received different sanction. 2025 Code (item 10): inconsistency does not automatically render a dismissal unfair where the misconduct makes continued employment intolerable.
decision_factors.template_yes: The rule has been consistently applied by the employer. No comparable case of similar misconduct has been treated differently, or the following cases were treated consistently: [describe].
decision_factors.template_inconsistent: There was inconsistency in that [describe], however this inconsistency does not render the dismissal unfair because the conduct in the present case rendered the continued employment relationship intolerable, which distinguishes it from the prior case(s). Authority: Code of Good Practice: Dismissal (2025), item 10.

decision_factors.label: What was the actual or potential harm?
decision_factors.guidance: NEW FACTOR — 2025 Code, item 9. Consider: financial, reputational, safety, or relational harm resulting from the misconduct, or harm that could have resulted. Greater harm supports a more serious sanction.
decision_factors.template_significant: The misconduct caused / had the potential to cause the following significant harm to the employer / fellow employees / third parties: [describe]. This significant harm / risk of harm supports the imposition of a more serious sanction.
decision_factors.template_minor: The misconduct caused limited actual harm, in that [describe]. The potential for harm was [moderate / low]. This factor [does not support / weighs against] the most severe available sanction.

decision_factors.label: Is dismissal proportionate? Could progressive discipline prevent recurrence?
decision_factors.guidance: The 2025 Code requires consideration of whether progressive discipline might prevent recurrence. If a warning could achieve the corrective purpose, dismissal is not appropriate. Dismissal is appropriate where the misconduct is so serious that it renders continued employment intolerable, or where progressive discipline has already failed.
decision_factors.template_dismissal: I have considered whether progressive discipline could prevent a recurrence of this misconduct. Having regard to [the nature and severity of the misconduct / the prior warning history / the irreparable breach of trust], I find that a lesser sanction would not achieve the corrective purpose of discipline. Dismissal is the appropriate and proportionate sanction.
decision_factors.template_warning: I have considered whether dismissal is proportionate. Having regard to [the employee's length of service / clean prior record / genuine remorse / the less serious nature of the conduct], I find that a [written warning / final written warning] is capable of achieving the corrective purpose of discipline, and dismissal would not be proportionate in the circumstances.

case_law.name: Sidumo v Rustenburg Platinum Mines (2007)
case_law.principle: Reasonable decision-maker test — the question is whether the dismissal was fair, not whether the arbitrator would have imposed the same sanction.

case_law.name: Edcon Ltd v Pillemer NO (2010)
case_law.principle: Prior warnings for the same conduct are a significant aggravating factor and may justify a more severe sanction than would otherwise apply.

case_law.name: National Employers General Insurance v Jagers (1984)
case_law.principle: Where two mutually destructive versions are presented, the court must apply its mind to all the evidence and decide which version is more probable.

case_law.name: Avril Elizabeth Home v CCMA (2006)
case_law.principle: A fair procedure requires genuine dialogue and an opportunity to respond, not strict procedural formality. The decriminalised approach to disciplinary hearings.

case_law.name: Murray & Roberts Cementation v AMCU obo Dube (2024)
case_law.principle: Confirmed principles for formulation of charge sheets; consistency does not require identical treatment where misconduct itself differs in material respects.

case_law.name: Samson v CCMA (2010)
case_law.principle: An employer may increase a sanction imposed at a disciplinary hearing where the company practice permits this and the increase is fair in the circumstances.

ccma.note: If a party fails to refer within 30 days, they must apply for condonation. Late referrals may not be accepted.
